repo_metadata.name: multica
repo_metadata.description: The open-source managed agents platform. Turn coding agents into real teammates — assign tasks, track progress, compound skills.

first_match.snippet: // before any child_process.spawn / execFile call in the main process —
- **自主执行** — 设置后无需管理。完整的任务生命周期管理（排队、认领、执行、完成/失败），通过 WebSocket 实时推送进度。
- **Autonomous Execution** — set it and forget it. Full task lifecycle management (enqueue, claim, start, complete/fail)
# Incremental fetch (only messages after a given sequence number)
| **Backend** | REST API + WebSocket server | Go (single binary) |
| `MULTICA_SERVER_URL` | `ws://localhost:8080/ws` | WebSocket URL for daemon → server connection |
// Capture URL passed to WebSocket so we can assert the connect-time
*  browsers cannot set custom headers on WebSocket handshakes, so query
// WebSocket event types (matching Go server protocol/events.go)
* @param ws - WebSocket client instance (null when not yet connected)
* Hook that subscribes to a WebSocket event and calls the handler.
- **流式响应**：通过 WebSocket 实时推送
| 17 | **Realtime & Events** | WebSocket hub + room model + 事件目录 | **40+ event types**（按命名空间分：issue:* / task:* / inbox:*
- WebSocket event 清单（v2 Realtime 页）
// WorkspaceID so it reaches frontend WebSocket clients (events without WorkspaceID

open_security_issues.body_snippet:
## Summary

The `/auth/verify-code` endpoint does not implement any rate-limiting, lockout, or exponential backoff for brute-force verification attempts. An attacker can guess the 6-digit master code indefinitely without any penalties.

## Version

multica-api v0.2.16

## Reproducer

```bash
# Send
open_security_issues.title: [Security] v0.2.16: /auth/verify-code lacks brute-force protection — no lockout or backoff

open_security_issues.author: shaun0927
open_security_issues.body_snippet:
### Deployment type

Self-hosted

### What happened?

`isBlockedEnvKey` in `server/internal/daemon/daemon.go` (v0.2.0, lines 1380–1388) only rejects `HOME`, `PATH`, `USER`, `SHELL`, `TERM`, `CODEX_HOME`, and anything prefixed with `MULTICA_`. Several well-known variables that lead to code execution
open_security_issues.title: [Bug]: Security: isBlockedEnvKey allows LD_PRELOAD/NODE_OPTIONS/HTTP_PROXY override via custom_env

open_security_issues.author: skip-agent
open_security_issues.body_snippet:
## Summary

The Multica API does not validate the `Origin` header on state-changing requests (e.g. `POST /api/issues`). An attacker can spoof the `Origin` header to an arbitrary domain, and as long as a valid `X-CSRF-Token` and session cookie are present, the request is accepted.

## Version

multic
open_security_issues.title: [Security] v0.2.16: Origin header not validated on state-changing requests — CSRF defense-in-depth gap

entries.title: Self-hosted hardening relies on operator preserving APP_ENV=production gate that disables the 888888 dev verification code
entries.action_hint: If self-hosting, do NOT change APP_ENV from `production`. Verify your `docker compose ... config` shows `APP_ENV=production` before going live; treat any non-production APP_ENV as a public-instance disqualifier until MULTICA_DEV_VERIFICATION_CODE is also empty.
entries.threat_model_context: The `MULTICA_DEV_VERIFICATION_CODE` environment variable, when set to a six-digit value (the docs use `888888` as the example), allows anyone who can request a verification code to log in as the requested email. The bypass is gated by `APP_ENV=production`: server/cmd/server/main.go:128 logs a warning and skips activation when production; auth.go:97-108 isDevVerificationCode() short-circuits to false in production. docker-compose.selfhost.yml pins `APP_ENV: ${APP_ENV:-production}` by default, so the out-of-the-box self-host posture is safe. The risk is operator error: an admin who sets APP_ENV to 'staging' or 'dev' (commonly done when running a private test instance) AND has MULTICA_DEV_VERIFICATION_CODE set, runs an instance where 888888 logs in as anyone. The 2026-04-19 prior scan flagged this as Critical at HEAD b8907dd; the APP_ENV gate has been added since (closed issue #1304 documents the fix). The current posture is Warning — defended by default, but the defense is one config flip away from being absent.
entries.what_this_means: Out-of-the-box Docker self-host is hardened. The 888888 master-code path only opens up if an operator deliberately turns off APP_ENV=production AND populates the dev code variable.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not the same finding the prior 2026-04-19 scan flagged as Critical. That scan caught the bypass before APP_ENV gating was in place. Current code requires explicit double-mistake (non-production env + dev code populated) to expose the bypass.
entries.date_label: Since hardening landed (~2026-04-18, per closed issue #1304)
entries.how_to_fix: Operator-side: keep `APP_ENV=production` in your environment / .env. Leave `MULTICA_DEV_VERIFICATION_CODE` empty in production. If you need a test instance accessible from the internet, use a proper provider OAuth path (Google) instead of email codes and unset the dev code variable entirely. Maintainer-side: consider gating MULTICA_DEV_VERIFICATION_CODE on build-time (only compiled into dev builds) so the production binary cannot enable it under any runtime configuration.

entries.title: curl-pipe-from-main installer with no checksum verification on macOS/Linux (Windows installer DOES verify)
entries.action_hint: Prefer the Homebrew install path (`brew install multica-ai/tap/multica`) — formula-pinned and checksum-verified. If you must use the curl|bash path, download the script first and read it before piping to bash.
entries.threat_model_context: Two install paths in the README run shell from the network: `curl -fsSL .../main/scripts/install.sh | bash` and `irm .../main/scripts/install.ps1 | iex`. Both fetch from the `main` branch (no SHA or tag pin). Compromise paths: (a) any push to main that touches scripts/install.sh ships immediately to every new installer; (b) the install.sh binary-download step (lines 83-130) does NOT verify a SHA256 checksum — it scrapes the redirect on `releases/latest` to learn the tag, downloads the binary from `releases/download/<tag>/...`, and `mv`s it to /usr/local/bin without integrity check. install.ps1 by contrast (lines 195-213) downloads `checksums.txt` from the release and validates SHA256. Result: Windows users get checksum verification; Linux/macOS users do not. Either a release-asset replacement attack OR a redirect tampering OR a tag move bypasses no-pin install on Unix; on Windows the checksums.txt would catch it (assuming checksums.txt is itself trusted, which it is here only because GitHub Releases assets are append-only per release tag).
entries.what_this_means: On macOS/Linux, the installer trusts whatever binary lives at the release URL. There is no second-channel verification. On Windows, the installer adds a SHA256 check via checksums.txt — meaningfully tighter.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence the installer has been tampered with. The asymmetry between install.sh and install.ps1 looks like a feature gap (Windows installer was hardened later) rather than malicious. The Homebrew path remains the lowest-risk install method because Homebrew formulae are tap-pinned and pull from a separate distribution channel with its own integrity checks.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add `checksums.txt` download + sha256sum verification to install.sh on the Unix path (mirror the install.ps1 pattern at lines 195-213). Optionally pin the install script to a release tag in the README curl command (`raw.githubusercontent.com/multica-ai/multica/v0.2.X/scripts/install.sh`) so install.sh updates are gated on tagged releases rather than every push to main. Consumer-side: prefer Homebrew where available; otherwise download install.sh first (`curl -O https://...`), read it, then `bash install.sh`.

entries.title: Branch-protection ruleset present-but-disabled, no CODEOWNERS, no enforced rules-on-default — visible-but-toothless governance posture
entries.action_hint: Read open security issues (4 as of scan) before pulling; treat any cherry-picked direct-to-main commit as ungated until the `protect-main-branch` ruleset enforcement flips on.
entries.threat_model_context: All four canonical governance signals are negative: classic protection HTTP 404, ruleset count 1 BUT `enforcement: disabled`, rules-on-default 0, no CODEOWNERS at any of 4 standard paths. A ruleset named `protect-main-branch` was created 2026-01-15 but never enabled — it is visible-but-toothless. Org rulesets unknown (token scope). 84 contributors with the top contributor at 29.7% commit share — NOT solo-maintained, so the bus-factor angle is weaker than the 2026-04-19 scan suggested. The C20 governance computer fires Warning rather than Critical because there are no formal releases (the 'recent release amplifier' that promotes governance-SPOF to Critical for shipping-binaries shape is N/A here — multica ships via GHCR images and an as-yet-unpublished Homebrew tap, but no GitHub Releases at scan time). Risk path: a maintainer push to main (intentional or via stolen token) reaches `multica setup` users via the curl-pipe-from-main installer with no second-human gate.
entries.what_this_means: Multica has the governance scaffold (a ruleset entry exists) but enforcement is off. The visible ruleset reads as 'we know we should have protection' — the intent is documented; the enforcement is not.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not solo-maintained. 84 contributors have committed; top contributor share is 29.7%, well below the 80% solo threshold. The risk is a process gap, not a single-person SPOF.
entries.date_label: Since repo created (2026-01-13); ruleset added 2026-01-15 disabled
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: flip the existing `protect-main-branch` ruleset enforcement from `disabled` to `active` and require ≥1 review on PRs to main. Add a CODEOWNERS file (even self-listed) so reviewers are auto-requested. Add SECURITY.md to declare a disclosure channel. Consumer-side: pin to a release tag in install paths (`MULTICA_SELFHOST_REF=v0.2.X` env var) once releases ship, rather than tracking main.

entries.title: 5.7% formal/any review rate over 300 closed PRs; 220 of 300 (73%) self-merged
entries.action_hint: Don't assume the next merged commit was reviewed by a second person — most aren't. Inspect security-relevant PR diffs directly when triaging an upgrade.
entries.threat_model_context: 300-PR sample yields 5.7% formal review rate AND 5.7% any-review rate (formal === any in the sample, suggesting reviews-when-they-happen are formal approvals; the issue is they rarely happen). 220/300 self-merged (73%). 30 PRs in the sample carried security keywords. The combination — F2's missing enforcement + F3's de-facto self-merge norm — means most code reaching main has had no second-human read, including security-relevant changes. This is the operational shape of F2; it is itself a finding because the gap is observable in PR data (independent of the governance signals).
entries.what_this_means: When multica's main branch advances by a commit, the realistic expectation is 'someone on the team pushed this directly or self-merged a PR they opened minutes ago' — not 'a reviewer signed off.'
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of intentional review-bypass. With 84 contributors and many small fix-PRs, self-merge norms can develop organically when CI is the primary gate. The risk is structural (no human gate), not behavioral.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: enabling the `protect-main-branch` ruleset (see F2 fix) closes both findings — required-review-on-PR forces formal review, blocks self-merge. Consumer-side: when picking an install pin, choose a tagged release rather than `main`-tracking once releases exist.

entries.title: 4 open security issues (oldest 16 days) including a privilege-escalation report on agent custom_env handling
entries.action_hint: Before deploying multica with externally-controlled agent runtimes, read issue #1114 and decide whether the LD_PRELOAD bypass affects your trust model.
entries.threat_model_context: Open security issues at scan: #1114 (16d, no fix yet) — `isBlockedEnvKey allows LD_PRELOAD/NODE_OPTIONS/HTTP_PROXY override via custom_env`, a privilege-escalation in agent execution where a workspace can pass loader-overriding env vars to spawned agent CLIs; #1382 (12d) vague 'security vulnerability report' (labeled `question`); #1663 (7d) /auth/verify-code lacks brute-force protection (no lockout / backoff); #1667 (7d) Origin header not validated on state-changing requests (CSRF defense-in-depth gap). Counter-signal: the team closed FIVE security issues in the prior 8 days (2026-04-16 to 2026-04-24) — the 888888 self-host issue, shell.openExternal IPC URL-scheme bypass, Hermes filterCustomArgs bypass, /health/realtime metrics exposure, and open-redirect via ?next=. Pattern: responsive (5 closed in 8 days), but the queue is not shrinking (4 still open). The 16-day-old #1114 is the most concerning — privilege escalation in the core agent-execution flow, and unresolved past two weeks.
entries.what_this_means: Multica's security backlog is short and active. The team fixes things on a one-to-two-week cadence. The open items are real (especially #1114), but none are unbounded-old.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not 'unresponsive maintainers.' The closed-issue list shows a team actively triaging and fixing security reports. The 4-open backlog is the working set, not a graveyard.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: prioritize #1114 (the LD_PRELOAD/NODE_OPTIONS bypass is a real privesc on the agent-execution path). Add SECURITY.md so reporters know the disclosure channel and labeled issues. Consumer-side: if you're running multica in any context where workspace operators are not 100% trusted, watch #1114 before wiring in custom_env.

entries.title: 1 known transitive vulnerability: @tiptap/extension-link (GHSA-vhrc-hgrq-x75r); no Dependabot config; alerts API behind admin scope
entries.action_hint: Watch for tiptap upstream patch; consider enabling Dependabot in your fork if you self-host.
entries.threat_model_context: osv.dev batch-lookup over 30 declared deps yielded one advisory: @tiptap/extension-link (GHSA-vhrc-hgrq-x75r — DOM XSS in tiptap's link extension). Single low-severity transitive vuln. Direct dep count: 322 runtime + 122 dev. The native Dependabot alerts API returned HTTP 403 (token scope `admin:org` required to read alerts) so the true alert state is unknown. No `.github/dependabot.yml` is present in the repo — Dependabot is not configured, even at the alert-only tier. SECURITY.md absent (E11).
entries.what_this_means: One known low-severity transitive XSS in tiptap. Not a structural finding — it's an upstream fix-and-bump. The absence of Dependabot config is a defensibility gap (no automatic alert channel).
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: The 403 on the Dependabot alerts API is a token-scope issue, NOT evidence Dependabot is disabled at the org level. The true alert count is unknown from outside.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add `.github/dependabot.yml` enabling at minimum the npm ecosystem on the root + monorepo packages. Consumer-side: noop for now — single low-sev XSS in a UI library is not a deployment blocker.

entries.title: Scanner coverage gaps: OSSF Scorecard not indexed; gitleaks not installed; npm registry probe unavailable for 10/10 declared packages
entries.action_hint: Re-run with admin-scoped GitHub token + gitleaks installed for full coverage; check npm registry directly to confirm @multica/* packages are not yet published.
entries.threat_model_context: Coverage limitations independent of the repo: (1) OSSF Scorecard returned HTTP 404 — the repo is not yet indexed (4-month-old repos often aren't). (2) gitleaks was not available in the scan environment, so secrets-in-history were not scanned. (3) npm registry presence probe returned `unavailable` for all 10 declared @multica/* packages — likely they haven't been published to npm yet (registry-presence:'unavailable' is the harness's signal for either not-published or registry-probe-failed; manual `npm view multica` would disambiguate). (4) Dependabot alerts API 403 (admin scope). (5) Org rulesets API error (admin:org scope). These are scanner-side limitations, not findings about multica.
entries.what_this_means: Some signals are 'unknown' rather than 'absent.' Specifically: secrets-in-git-history, true Dependabot alert count, org-level rulesets, and confirmation of npm package publication status.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence anything is wrong with multica. Listed as Info so consumers know what the scan did NOT cover.
entries.how_to_fix: Re-run with: (a) `gh auth refresh -s admin:org` to enable org rulesets + Dependabot alerts; (b) `brew install gitleaks` (or equivalent) to enable secrets-in-history scan; (c) `npm view multica` to confirm npm registry status.

section_01.pill_row: 23.5k⭐ · NOASSERTION · TS+Go monorepo · 5 warnings · 1 info · caution
section_01.summary: multica-ai/multica is a 4-month-old open-source agentic platform — assign coding tasks to AI agents the way you'd assign to a teammate. The repo is a pnpm + Turborepo TypeScript monorepo (apps/web Next.js, apps/desktop Electron, packages/{core,ui,views,eslint-config,tsconfig}) plus a Go server (server/) and a CLI binary built via GoReleaser. 84 contributors with the top contributor at 29.7% commit share — actively multi-maintainer. The verdict is Caution rather than Critical because (a) the previously-flagged 888888 self-host bypass has been APP_ENV-gated since ~2026-04-18 (closing the 2026-04-19 prior scan's Critical concern); (b) 5 security issues have been closed in the past 8 days, indicating active triage; (c) 0 pull_request_target workflows means no PR-target attack surface. The Caution applies primarily to install-path discipline (curl|bash on Unix without checksums), the visible-but-disabled branch-protection ruleset, and the 16-day-old open privesc report (#1114).

section_02.pill_row: F0 Warning · F1 Warning · F2 Warning · F3 Warning · F4 Warning · F5 Info · F6 Info

section_06.summary: **Asterisk on Q2** — calibration v2 advisory was red; Phase 4 overrode to amber on `missing_qualitative_context` because the team has closed 5 security issues in the past 8 days at roughly the same rate the open queue is being filed. The override is documented with `override_reason='missing_qualitative_context'` per Gate 6.3.

split_axis_decision.reasoning: Multica has two distinct consumer profiles with materially different risk: (1) CLI-only consumer using the cloud (multica.ai/app) — the `multica setup` flow with Homebrew install, never touches the self-host bypass paths; (2) Self-host operator who runs `--with-server` and operates the Docker stack — exposed to the F0 APP_ENV configuration discipline + all the install-path concerns + open #1114 LD_PRELOAD bypass concerns. The verdicts diverge in degree (both Caution, but the self-host operator carries more responsibility for getting config right).

entries.headline: Use Homebrew; review CLI updates' release notes; the cloud-side has no exposure to the self-host bypass paths.
Do NOT change APP_ENV from production. Pin to a release tag once releases ship. Watch issue #1114 before exposing custom_env to untrusted workspace operators.

groups.headline: Caution — 5 Warning findings cluster around process gaps + install-path asymmetry
groups.one_line_summary: 23.5k-star agentic-platform monorepo, 4 months old, 84 contributors but 5.7% review rate and 73% self-merge. Governance ruleset exists but enforcement is off. Curl-pipe installer skips checksums on Unix.

items.claim: Self-host 888888 dev-code path is APP_ENV=production-gated by default (improved from prior scan); operator-error scenarios re-open it.
install.sh on macOS/Linux skips SHA256 verification; install.ps1 on Windows does verify.
Branch-protection ruleset exists but `enforcement: disabled`. No CODEOWNERS, no SECURITY.md.
5.7% formal review rate; 220 of 300 sample PRs self-merged. The operational shape of F2.
4 open security issues (#1114 16-day LD_PRELOAD privesc); 5 fixed in past 8 days — responsive but not shrinking.

groups.headline: Info — F5 + F6 are upstream-tracking + scanner coverage notes
groups.one_line_summary: 1 known low-severity transitive XSS; OSSF/gitleaks/npm-registry coverage gaps logged.

items.claim: @tiptap/extension-link GHSA-vhrc-hgrq-x75r — single low-severity transitive vuln. No Dependabot config.
Scanner did not run gitleaks; OSSF Scorecard not indexed; 10/10 npm packages registry-status `unavailable`.

selections.reason: F2 governance posture is the upstream cause of F3's review-rate signal. Flipping the existing `protect-main-branch` ruleset enforcement from `disabled` to `active` is the single highest-value remediation — would close F2 directly and most of F3 indirectly.

Counter-evidence to F4's open-backlog framing — 5 security issues closed in past 8 days. Without E5, Q2 stays red; with E5, the team's responsiveness is visible and Q2 drops to amber with override_reason='missing_qualitative_context'.
F1 install-path asymmetry is most actionable as a maintainer fix: mirror install.ps1's checksums.txt verification into install.sh (~30 lines). Closes F1 directly.

entries.headline: If using cloud (multica.ai), install via Homebrew
entries.non_technical: The cloud path is the simplest and lowest-risk install: Homebrew tap with formula-pinning + checksum verification. After install, `multica setup` connects you to the cloud workspace.

entries.headline: If using curl|bash on Unix, download and read install.sh first
entries.non_technical: The README's curl|bash path on macOS/Linux skips SHA256 verification on the downloaded binary. Mitigation: download the script, read it, then bash it — or just use Homebrew (step 1) where possible.

entries.headline: If self-hosting, verify APP_ENV=production before exposing the instance
entries.non_technical: The 888888 dev-verification-code path is disabled when APP_ENV=production. Docker self-host pins APP_ENV=production by default, but if you've inherited a non-default .env (e.g., from a guide that suggested setting APP_ENV=staging for dev), the bypass re-opens. Confirm before publishing the URL.
entries.command: cd ~/.multica/server && docker compose -f docker-compose.selfhost.yml config | grep APP_ENV

entries.headline: If wiring custom_env into agent runtimes, read open issue #1114
entries.non_technical: Issue #1114 reports that the env-var allowlist for agent custom_env permits LD_PRELOAD/NODE_OPTIONS/HTTP_PROXY — which can let a workspace operator inject loader hooks into spawned agent CLIs. If your trust boundary includes 'workspace operators are not 100% trusted,' don't expose custom_env until #1114 lands.

executable_file_inventory.lead: multica is a TypeScript monorepo + Go server + Electron desktop. The execution surface fans across 3 runtimes: (1) Go binary `multica` CLI (~500 LOC of cmd/ + handler/ + daemon/ in server/); (2) Node.js apps/web (Next.js App Router); (3) Electron apps/desktop. Plus 2 install scripts (install.sh 462 lines, install.ps1 483 lines) that the README pipes through bash/iex.
executable_file_inventory.layer1_summary: Primary surface: Go server (server/ — cmd/server/main.go entrypoint + internal/handler + internal/daemon). Secondary surface: Next.js web app (apps/web). Tertiary surface: Electron desktop (apps/desktop). Distribution surface: scripts/install.sh (Unix), scripts/install.ps1 (Windows), Dockerfile + Dockerfile.web, docker-compose.selfhost.yml, .goreleaser.yml.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: curl-pipe-from-main; binary download via redirect-scrape; mv to /usr/local/bin without checksum (lines 83-130). git clone + git checkout in --with-server mode (lines 271-339).
layer2_entries.network: Yes — fetches binary tarballs from GitHub release CDN; clones repo for self-host mode.
layer2_entries.notes: set -euo pipefail (defensive). Detects OS/arch. Falls back to ~/.local/bin if /usr/local/bin not writable. NO checksum verification on binary download — the asymmetry vs install.ps1 is F1.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: Invoke-Expression (`iex`) entry point per README. Invoke-WebRequest for binary + checksums. Expand-Archive to %LOCALAPPDATA%.
layer2_entries.network: Yes — fetches binary zip + checksums.txt from GitHub release CDN.
layer2_entries.notes: Verifies SHA256 against checksums.txt (lines 195-213). The Unix install.sh does not have this — F1 asymmetry.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: Configures HTTP server, JWT auth, agent runtime daemons. The 888888 dev-code env-var consumption is gated at line 128 by APP_ENV=production check.
layer2_entries.network: Yes — listens on :8080 by default; outbound to Resend (email), Google OAuth, optional S3.
layer2_entries.notes: The APP_ENV=production gate at line 128 is the F0 defense. JWT_SECRET defaults to `change-me-in-production` if unset (warning logged). Generates JWT signing key per build.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: isDevVerificationCode() at line 97 — the 888888 path. Short-circuits to false in production (isProductionEnv check). Constant-time comparison at line 107.
layer2_entries.network: Email-code send via Resend; verify-code endpoint accepts user-submitted codes.
layer2_entries.notes: Open issue #1663 reports lack of brute-force protection on /auth/verify-code (no lockout/backoff). Open issue #1667 reports Origin-header CSRF defense gap.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: JWT_SECRET defaults to insecure literal if unset (line 44). APP_ENV pinned to 'production' by default (line 58 — F0 defense).
layer2_entries.network: Pulls ghcr.io/multica-ai/multica-{backend,web} images; tag default `latest` per .env.example.
layer2_entries.notes: install.sh setup_server() generates a random JWT_SECRET via `openssl rand -hex 32` on first install (line 304), patching the insecure default at install time.

executable_file_inventory.closing_note: Two install-path entrypoints (install.sh / install.ps1) plus the Go server binary handle the consumer-facing risk. The TypeScript monorepo (apps/web + packages/*) is large but its risk profile is dominated by upstream npm deps (E11) rather than novel surface.

entries: OSSF Scorecard — not_indexed: Repo not yet indexed by OSSF (4 months old). Will appear once the project crosses scorecard's discovery threshold.
Secrets-in-history (gitleaks) — not_available: gitleaks not installed in scan environment; install + re-run for git-history secret coverage.
Dependabot alert count — unknown: API returned HTTP 403 (admin scope required). Re-run with `gh auth refresh -s admin:org`.
Org rulesets — unknown: API errored (admin:org scope required). Re-run with elevated token to confirm whether an org-level ruleset enforces protection that the repo-level one does not.

npm registry status (10 packages) — unavailable: Harness reported `unavailable` for all 10 declared @multica/* packages. Likely not yet published — confirm with `npm view multica` and `npm view @multica/core`.

does_anyone_check_the_code.short_answer: No — 5.7% review rate, 73% self-merge, branch-protection ruleset declared but enforcement disabled, no CODEOWNERS
does_anyone_check_the_code.rationale: 300-PR sample shows 5.7% formal review and 220/300 self-merges. The `protect-main-branch` ruleset exists (id 11828157, created 2026-01-15) but `enforcement: disabled` — the governance scaffold is visible without being active (E2, E3). With no CODEOWNERS at any of 4 standard paths, there is no automatic reviewer assignment. Aligned with library-package advisory (FALLBACK rule fires red on these signals).

do_they_fix_problems_quickly.short_answer: Mixed — 5 security issues closed in past 8 days (responsive), but 4 still open with oldest 16 days (privesc unaddressed)
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.rationale: Calibration v2 advisory says red (4 open security items, oldest 16 days). Override to amber: counter-signal is strong — the team CLOSED 5 security issues in the prior 8 calendar days (888888 self-host, shell.openExternal, Hermes filterCustomArgs, /health/realtime exposure, open-redirect ?next=) — see E5. The fix rate roughly matches the open-arrival rate, indicating responsiveness rather than backlog growth. The unresolved #1114 LD_PRELOAD bypass (16 days) is the strongest dissent; without it, this would lean green.
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.edge_case: Counter-signal — rapid security-issue close rate offsets open-side count.
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.suggested_threshold_adjustment: Q2 calibration could weight 'closed_within_window' as a counter-signal — currently the open-side counts dominate even when the close-side is equally active.

do_they_tell_you_about_problems.short_answer: No — no SECURITY.md, no published advisories, no Dependabot config; reporters use generic issues
do_they_tell_you_about_problems.rationale: community_profile.has_security_policy=False; no SECURITY.md at root; no .github/dependabot.yml; published_advisory_count=0 (E11). Reporters submit security findings as ordinary GitHub issues with `[Security]` titles — they get triaged, but there is no documented disclosure channel or commitment to handling embargoed reports. Aligned with library-package advisory red.

is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.short_answer: Partly — Homebrew path is safe; curl|bash path on macOS/Linux skips checksum verification (Windows installer does verify); APP_ENV=production gate defends the 888888 path by default
is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.rationale: Three install paths with different safety profiles (E6, E7): Homebrew tap is formula-pinned + checksum-verified (low risk); curl|bash on Unix tracks main and skips SHA256 verification on the binary download (medium risk — F1); install.ps1 on Windows DOES verify checksums.txt. Self-host hardening: docker-compose.selfhost.yml pins APP_ENV=production by default, which disables the dev verification code path (E8 — F0 fix landed ~2026-04-18). Net: the default install path consumers will land on (Homebrew if present, else curl|bash) is medium-risk on Unix without operator action.

catalog_metadata.short_description: Open-source managed agents platform — assign coding tasks to AI agents (Claude Code, Codex, Hermes, etc.) via web/desktop dashboard. TS monorepo + Go server + Electron desktop, distributed via Homebrew + curl|bash + GHCR Docker images. By multica-ai (organization, 84 contributors).
catalog_metadata.target_user: Developer or small AI-native team adopting agent-managed task workflows. Cloud users hit `brew install multica-ai/tap/multica` then `multica setup`. Self-host operators run `curl ... install.sh | bash -s -- --with-server` then `multica setup self-host`.
catalog_metadata.shape: Agentic platform (server + CLI + web + desktop). Multi-runtime: Go backend, Next.js web, Electron desktop, Node CLI. Closest V1.2 catalog comparator: none — first agentic-platform shape in V1.2 (Archon was V2.4-era). Calibration v2 classified as `library-package` due to publishable npm manifest, with Phase 4 overrides on Q2.
catalog_metadata.prior_scan_note: Re-scan of multica-ai/multica. Catalog entry 11 was scanned 2026-04-19 at HEAD b8907dd (V2.4 / delegated, Caution split). Current scan: V2.5-preview at HEAD 3df95c8 (16 days later, +4 commits visible in PR cadence). Verdict still Caution; the 888888 dev-code Critical concern from prior scan is now Warning because APP_ENV gate landed (closed issue #1304, ~2026-04-18). Open #1114 LD_PRELOAD privesc is new vs prior scan.

editorial_caption.text: multica-ai/multica — 23.5k-star open-source agentic platform (TS+Go monorepo, Electron desktop, GHCR self-host), 4 months old, 84 contributors. The 888888 dev-verification-code Critical from the 2026-04-19 prior scan is now Warning (APP_ENV=production gate landed ~2026-04-18). Active responsiveness — 5 security issues closed in past 8 days. Open concerns: branch-protection ruleset declared but enforcement disabled (5.7% review rate, 73% self-merge), curl|bash installer skips SHA256 verification on macOS/Linux (Windows DOES verify), and #1114 LD_PRELOAD privesc on agent custom_env still open at 16 days. Caution.

body_paragraphs: The 2026-04-19 prior scan flagged this as Critical: any operator running self-host with the dev verification code populated could log in as anyone via the literal `888888`. Since that scan, multica has added an APP_ENV=production gate in two places: server/cmd/server/main.go:128 logs a warning and skips activation when production; auth.go:97-108 isDevVerificationCode() short-circuits to false in production. Closed issue #1304 documents the fix.

docker-compose.selfhost.yml pins `APP_ENV: ${APP_ENV:-production}` by default, so the out-of-the-box self-host posture is hardened. The remaining Warning-level risk is operator error: an admin who deliberately sets APP_ENV to non-production (commonly done when running a private test instance) AND populates MULTICA_DEV_VERIFICATION_CODE re-opens the bypass.

The mitigation is config discipline rather than code change. The defense is one config flip away from being absent — which is why this is Warning rather than OK. A maintainer-side hardening would gate the dev-code variable at build-time, so the production binary cannot enable it under any runtime configuration.

Two install paths in the README run shell from the network. `curl -fsSL .../main/scripts/install.sh | bash` on macOS/Linux. `irm .../main/scripts/install.ps1 | iex` on Windows. Both fetch from the `main` branch (no SHA pin, no tag pin). install.sh's binary-download step (lines 83-130) scrapes the redirect on `releases/latest` to learn the tag, downloads the binary, and `mv`s it to /usr/local/bin without integrity check.

install.ps1 by contrast (lines 195-213) downloads `checksums.txt` from the release tag and validates SHA256 of the downloaded zip. Windows users get checksum verification; Linux/macOS users do not. The asymmetry reads as a feature gap (Windows installer was hardened later) rather than malicious — but it leaves the largest install audience (Unix) unverified.

The Homebrew install path (`brew install multica-ai/tap/multica`) sidesteps both. Homebrew formulae are tap-pinned and pull from a separate distribution channel with its own integrity checks. Whenever Homebrew is available, prefer it.

All four canonical governance signals are negative. Classic protection HTTP 404. Ruleset count 1 — but `enforcement: disabled` on the only ruleset (`protect-main-branch`, id 11828157, created 2026-01-15 and never enabled). Rules-on-default 0. CODEOWNERS not present at any of 4 standard paths. Org rulesets unknown (token scope).

The presence of a disabled ruleset is the most informative signal — the maintainers KNOW they should have branch protection (they created the rule) but have not flipped enforcement on. Whether this is intentional (perhaps to avoid friction during the 4-month bootstrap) or oversight, the operational result is identical: every push to main reaches consumers with no second-human gate.

84 contributors with the top contributor at 29.7% commit share — multica is NOT solo-maintained, so the bus-factor angle is weaker than the prior scan's framing. The risk is procedural (no enforced gate), not single-person SPOF. The C20 governance computer fires Warning rather than Critical because there are no formal releases yet (the recent-release amplifier that promotes governance-SPOF to Critical is N/A; multica ships via GHCR images and an as-yet-unpublished Homebrew tap).

300-PR sample yields 5.7% formal review rate, 5.7% any-review rate, and 220 of 300 PRs (73%) self-merged. Lifetime merged: 1,026 PRs. The `formal === any` equality is interesting — when reviews happen, they are formal approvals; the issue is they rarely happen.

30 of the 300 PRs in the sample carried security keywords (title or path). Combined with F2's missing enforcement, the operational shape is: most code reaching main has had no second-human read, including security-relevant changes. This is the lived consequence of F2 — a finding in its own right because the gap is observable in PR data independent of governance signals.

The fix is the same as F2: enabling the existing `protect-main-branch` ruleset with required-review-on-PR closes both findings. The maintainers have the scaffold; flipping enforcement on is a one-action change.

Four security issues open at scan. The notable one is #1114 (16 days old): `isBlockedEnvKey allows LD_PRELOAD/NODE_OPTIONS/HTTP_PROXY override via custom_env`. The reporter walks through the agent-execution code path where workspace operators can pass loader-overriding env vars to spawned agent CLIs, escalating from workspace-operator privilege to whatever the agent CLI runs with. Unresolved past two weeks. The other three open items (#1382 vague report, #1663 brute-force protection, #1667 Origin/CSRF) are 7-12 days old.

Counter-signal: the team CLOSED FIVE security issues in the prior 8 days (2026-04-16 to 2026-04-24) — the 888888 self-host issue (#1304), shell.openExternal IPC URL-scheme bypass (#1115), Hermes filterCustomArgs bypass (#1113), /health/realtime metrics exposure (#1606), and open-redirect via ?next= (#1116). The fix rate roughly matches the open-arrival rate.

Pattern: responsive (5 closed in 8 days), but the queue is not shrinking. This is the operational shape of an active small-team project under active security-research scrutiny — the scrutiny is real (probably from the prior scan + community attention to agentic platforms), and the team is keeping up. The scorecard's Q2 advisory says red on the open-side count alone; the override to amber reflects the close-side counter-signal.

osv.dev batch lookup over 30 declared deps yielded one advisory: @tiptap/extension-link GHSA-vhrc-hgrq-x75r (DOM XSS in tiptap link extension). Single low-severity transitive vuln in a UI library — not a deployment blocker, just a fix-and-bump waiting on upstream tiptap.

Defensibility gap: no `.github/dependabot.yml` is present. Even at the alert-only tier, Dependabot is not configured. The native Dependabot alerts API returned HTTP 403 (admin scope required) so the true alert state inside the maintainer view is unknown — but the absence of a config file means there's no automated bump-PR pipeline.

Five coverage limitations independent of the repo: OSSF Scorecard returned HTTP 404 (repo not yet indexed; will appear once Scorecard's discovery threshold is crossed). gitleaks not installed in the scan environment, so secrets-in-history were not scanned. Dependabot alerts API 403 (admin scope). Org rulesets API errored (admin:org scope). npm registry presence probe `unavailable` for all 10 declared @multica/* packages — likely they haven't been published to npm yet, but the harness can't disambiguate that from registry-probe-failed.

Re-running with admin-scoped GitHub token + gitleaks installed + manual `npm view multica` would close all five gaps. None of these are findings about multica — they're scanner-side limits that consumers should know about so they can request a wider re-scan if needed.
